name: Ovidio Guzmán López
role: Los Chapitos Co-Leader, Sinaloa Cartel; arrested Jan 2023, extradited Sep 2023
quote: No comment — held in U.S. federal custody. Pleaded guilty July 2025 to drug trafficking and continuing criminal enterprise; sentencing July 2026.

name: Iván Archivaldo Guzmán Salazar
role: Los Chapitos Co-Leader ('El Chapito'); leads remaining Chapitos faction; $10M U.S. bounty
quote: Leads the Chapitos faction in the ongoing civil war against La Mayiza; formed tactical alliance with CJNG for weapons and manpower. Multiple U.S. indictments; at large as of early 2026.

name: Ismael 'El Mayo' Zambada García
role: Sinaloa Cartel Co-Founder; arrested El Paso July 2024; pleaded guilty Aug 2025
quote: I was kidnapped. Armed men in military uniforms put a bag over my head, zip-tied me, drugged me, and flew me to the United States against my will. — Per attorneys' account of El Mayo's statement, 2024

name: Joaquín Guzmán López
role: Los Chapitos Co-Leader ('El Ratón'); surrendered to U.S. El Paso July 2024; pleaded guilty Dec 2025
quote: Allegedly lured El Mayo to Culiacán under false pretenses and orchestrated his forcible transport to El Paso, then surrendered voluntarily. Pleaded guilty in Chicago to drug trafficking and continuing criminal enterprise.

name: Ismael Zambada Sicairos ('Mayito Flaco')
role: La Mayiza Leader; El Mayo's son; commands Sinaloa Cartel faction vs. Chapitos
quote: Authorized coordinated strikes against Los Chapitos on September 9, 2024, formally triggering the Sinaloa civil war. By early 2026, La Mayiza reportedly controls ~90% of former Chapitos territory.

name: Andrés Manuel López Obrador
role: President of Mexico (Dec 2018 – Sep 2024); ordered Ovidio's release in 2019
quote: La prioridad fue salvar vidas. (The priority was to save lives.) — Press conference, October 18, 2019, defending the order to release Ovidio Guzmán López

name: Luis Cresencio Sandoval González
role: SEDENA Secretary (2018–2024); oversaw both 2019 failure and 2023 Operation Ovidio
quote: La operación fue pobremente organizada, apresurada, y subestimó la fuerza y capacidad de movilización del Cártel de Sinaloa. (The operation was poorly organized, hasty, and underestimated the strength and mobilization capacity of the Sinaloa Cartel.) — Press conference, October 18, 2019

name: Alfonso Durazo Montaño
role: Secretary of Security and Citizen Protection (2018–2020); initially misrepresented the 2019 operation
quote: Acknowledged 'hasty planning, inadequate risk assessment, and bureaucratic oversights' after initially claiming the October 17 convoy was a 'routine patrol' — a claim contradicted within 24 hours.

name: Rosa Icela Rodríguez Velázquez
role: Secretary of Security and Citizen Protection (Nov 2020 – Sep 2024); first woman to hold post; served during 2023 Operation Ovidio
quote: Coordinated federal security response during the January 2023 Operation Ovidio. Currently serves as Secretary of the Interior under President Claudia Sheinbaum from October 2024.

name: Adán Augusto López Hernández
role: Secretary of the Interior under AMLO; declared order restored in Sinaloa on Jan 13, 2023
quote: El orden ha sido restablecido en Sinaloa. (Order has been reestablished in Sinaloa.) — January 13, 2023, eight days after Operation Ovidio began

name: Marcelo Ebrard Casaubon
role: Foreign Affairs Secretary under AMLO; confirmed 2019 operation was per U.S. extradition request
quote: 63 percent of the weapons seized in the January 2023 Culiacán operation originated in the United States — a finding that underscores the urgent need for U.S. action on firearms trafficking to Mexico. — Press statement, January 2023

name: Col. Juan José Moreno Orzua
role: Commander, 43rd Infantry Battalion; killed in Escuinapa ambush, January 5, 2023
quote: The most senior military fatality of Operation Ovidio. Ambushed by Los Chapitos hitmen with his four bodyguards near Escuinapa, Sinaloa, on January 5, 2023 at approximately 2:50 PM. His killing was a targeted cartel response to military operations.

name: Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo
role: President of Mexico (Oct 2024–present); inherited ongoing Sinaloa civil war and cartel fragmentation
quote: Inherited the Sinaloa Cartel civil war upon taking office October 1, 2024 — a conflict that produced nearly 2,000 homicides in one year. Her administration faces the challenge of cartel fragmentation without the concentrated leverage of targeting singular dominant leaders.
